notes: searches: ~11, clusters: 9 (scripture-fidelity, 1798/1260-historicist, Luther-eschatology, Joseph Wolff, England/Brock/Winter, Lacunza, Bengel, Gaussen, Scandinavian child-preachers, Millerite/1844 core). Delta format chosen: 127 claims risks a truncated full-file Write, and the delta touches no frozen fields (quote/claim_text/id). Doctrinal escalation applied to Millerite/1844 + worldwide-awakening cluster (academic anchors: Numbers & Butler eds. The Disappointed, Taylor Origins of Millerite Separatism, Wolff Oxford Modern Judaism article, Andrews JATS Lacunza). Control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims verified: GC-20-090, GC-20-106, GC-20-107.

GC-20-001.rationale: Prophetic interpretation of Revelation 14; not empirically checkable.

GC-20-002.rationale: Theological claim about divine design in the angel imagery; not empirically checkable.

GC-20-003.rationale: Interpretive reading of Revelation 14 imagery as evidence of a worldwide movement; theological.

GC-20-004.rationale: Prophetic interpretation (message = part of 'everlasting gospel' announcing judgment); theological.

GC-20-005.rationale: Theological claim about when the judgment message could be proclaimed; not empirically checkable.

GC-20-006.rationale: Interpretation of Daniel 12:4 ('seal to the time of the end'); theological.

GC-20-007.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation matches Daniel 12:4 KJV ('many shall run to and fro, and knowledge shall be increased') verbatim.

GC-20-008.rationale: [common_knowledge] Quotation matches 2 Thessalonians 2:3 KJV ('except there come a falling away first, and that man of sin be revealed').

GC-20-009.rationale: Theological inference from the 'man of sin' interpretation; not empirically checkable.

GC-20-010.rationale: Prophetic identification of the papacy as the 'man of sin' and the 1260-year scheme; historicist theology, not empirically decidable.

GC-20-011.rationale: [common_knowledge] The historical anchor is well documented: French general Berthier entered Rome and Pope Pius VI was taken prisoner in February 1798, a decisive blow to papal temporal power. The framing that this 'ended the 1260 years' is historicist interpretation shared with White's sources (Wylie, Andrews), not itself empirically decidable. source_quality: encyclopedic-only (textbook-level event; interpretive layer theological).

GC-20-012.rationale: Interpretive extension of Paul's caution to 1798; theological.

GC-20-013.rationale: Luther's Table Talk (c.1540) contains the statement that he expected the Last Day within about three hundred years ('it will not be absent full three hundred years longer'), placing it near 1840 — consistent with White's claim. Luther elsewhere also voiced imminence, so his eschatology was mixed; White accurately reports the specific 300-year statement. Counter-apologetic sites treating this as misrepresentation are logged as a position only.

sources.title: Martin Luther and the End of the World, Ministry Magazine (1951)
Luther's Eschatology, Oxford Handbook of Martin Luther's Theology

GC-20-014.rationale: Interpretive claim about the 'unsealing' of Daniel after 1798; theological.

GC-20-015.rationale: A transatlantic prophetic/millenarian revival did appear in multiple countries in the early 19th century (Wolff's global mission, Irving and the Albury conferences, Lacunza's influence, Württemberg Pietism, the Swedish revival, Millerism), broadly contemporaneous. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: Numbers & Butler (eds.), The Disappointed: Millerism and Millenarianism (Univ. Tennessee Press, 1993) — via Great Disappointment overview
Joseph Wolff (1795–1862)—The Making of an Englishman, Modern Judaism (Oxford)

GC-20-016.rationale: The claim that the movements arose 'solely by the study of the Scriptures' and independently overstates the historical picture: historians document substantial diffusion (Lacunza's book translated by Irving in 1827 and circulated in Britain, advent publications reprinted in England, Wolff's own worldwide circulation). Some genuinely independent study occurred, so the claim is partly true; the universal independence framing is the contested element. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: Two Hundred Years from Lacunza, JATS (Andrews Univ.) — Irving translation and transmission

GC-20-017.rationale: Wolff set out on his mission in 1821, roughly three years after Miller reached his prophetic exposition (c.1818). Dates consistent with biographical record and Wolff's own account.

GC-20-018.rationale: Wolff was born in Germany (Weilersbach, near Bamberg, 1795) of Jewish parentage; his father was a rabbi. Confirmed by the Jewish Encyclopedia and DNB.

GC-20-019.rationale: Wolff's early attraction to Christianity is documented (he left home 'on account of his inclination toward Christianity'); ultimately from his own account.

GC-20-020.rationale: Faithfully drawn from Wolff's own memoir (footnote 593). Autobiographical anecdote; not independently corroborable but accurately quoted per period source.

GC-20-021.rationale: Consistent with Wolff's documented early attraction to Christianity; from his self-report.

GC-20-022.rationale: Autobiographical anecdote from Wolff's memoir; accurately quoted, not independently verifiable.

GC-20-023.rationale: Faithfully drawn from Wolff's memoir (footnote 594); autobiographical.

GC-20-024.rationale: Wolff left home young to pursue education and religion; age 11 is his own account, consistent with DNB's 'leaving home on account of his inclination toward Christianity.'

GC-20-025.rationale: Wolff was led to Catholicism and baptized (Sept 1812) with the purpose of a mission to the Jews; documented.

GC-20-026.rationale: Wolff studied at the Collegio Romano and the Collegio di Propaganda in Rome (arrived 1816). Confirmed by Jewish Encyclopedia and DNB.

GC-20-027.rationale: Wolff publicly attacked church doctrine (notably papal infallibility) while at Rome. Documented.

GC-20-028.rationale: Initially received favorably (introduced to Pope Pius VII), Wolff was expelled from the papal dominions in 1818. Documented.

GC-20-029.rationale: Wolff was expelled and left free to travel; the 'incorrigible' characterization is his own framing but consistent with expulsion for 'erroneous opinions.'

GC-20-030.rationale: Wolff went to England and declared himself a member of the Church of England. Documented.

GC-20-031.rationale: After study in England Wolff set out on his mission in 1821; consistent with the record of his 1821–26 Levant travels.

GC-20-032.rationale: Faithful quotation from Wolff's published writings (footnote 595); corroborated by the insider source Wellcome, which reproduces Wolff's advent teaching. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — Wolff passages
sources.note: insider period source reproducing Wolff's writings

GC-20-033.rationale: Faithful quotation from Wolff's writings (footnote 596), consistent with his premillennial teaching as recorded by Wellcome. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874)

GC-20-035.rationale: Faithful quotation from Wolff's writings (footnote 598) presenting his 'signs of the times' argument; consistent with Wellcome's reproduction of Wolff. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874)

GC-20-036.rationale: Wolff expected the consummation within a very few years of Miller's date (Wolff looked toward c.1847); consistent with his published chronology. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874)

GC-20-037.rationale: Faithful quotation from Wolff's writings (footnote 599) criticizing allegorical/spiritualizing exegesis. Self-report.

GC-20-038.rationale: Wolff's extensive 1821–1845 travels through Egypt, Abyssinia, Palestine, Syria, Persia, Bokhara, and India are well documented (he styled himself apostle for Palestine, Persia, Bokhara and Balkh).

GC-20-039.rationale: Wolff visited the United States (1837); the St. Helena stopover is from his own itinerary account. Plausible and consistent with the record.

GC-20-040.rationale: Wolff's US visit is dated to 1837 (he preached before Congress in December 1837 and was ordained in New Jersey that year); the August 1837 New York arrival is from his own account and is consistent.

GC-20-041.rationale: Wolff preached in New York, Philadelphia, Baltimore and Washington; documented in DNB.

GC-20-042.rationale: Corroborated: former president John Quincy Adams moved that Wolff be granted the House hall for a lecture, and Wolff addressed Congress in 1837. The quotation is faithful to Wolff's memoir (footnote 600).

GC-20-043.rationale: Continuation of Wolff's own account (footnote 600); his 1837 US activity, including a New Jersey ordination, is documented. Honors from state legislatures are self-reported.

GC-20-044.rationale: Wolff travelled without European protection and endured serious perils (his Bokhara journey nearly ended in his execution). General characterization consistent with the record; details from his own narration.

GC-20-045.rationale: Self-reported superlatives from Wolff's memoirs (bastinadoed, starved, sold as a slave, three times condemned to death). His travels were genuinely dangerous, but these specific claims are uncorroborated and Wolff was known for colorful self-narration; treat with caution per extractor note.

GC-20-046.rationale: Consistent with the hazards of Wolff's routes; from his own account.

GC-20-047.rationale: Autobiographical incident from Wolff's memoirs; consistent with his hazardous mountain travels. Self-report.

GC-20-050.rationale: Wolff carried and distributed Bibles in Hebrew and other tongues; consistent with the record of his colportage. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — Wolff circulated many Bibles

GC-20-051.rationale: Wolff's mission did reach a very wide geographic range; 'a large part of the habitable globe' is hyperbolic but broadly matches his documented travels.

GC-20-052.rationale: Wolff distributed Scripture among Jews, Muslims (Turks), Parsees, Hindus and others while proclaiming the advent; corroborated by Wellcome. Self-report.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874)

GC-20-053.rationale: Faithful to Wolff's report (footnote 603) that Yemeni Arabs held a book ('Seera') foretelling Christ's coming and expected events in 1840; Wellcome reproduces Wolff's Yemen reports. Wolff's ethnographic claims are self-reported.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — Yemen/Wolff

GC-20-054.rationale: Faithful to Wolff's report (footnote 604) of the Rechabites and putative tribe-of-Dan in Yemen; Wellcome reproduces the same. The claim is that Wolff reported these, which is accurately quoted; the ethnographic identifications are his self-report and would be viewed skeptically by modern scholarship.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — Rechabites/Yemen

GC-20-055.rationale: A period missionary anecdote of a similar advent belief in Tartary; drawn from 19th-century missionary literature and not independently corroborable, but not contradicted.

GC-20-056.rationale: Same Tartary anecdote (a priest expecting Christ about 1844); uncorroborated period missionary report, not contradicted.

GC-20-057.rationale: A British prophetic/advent revival is documented from the mid-1820s (the Albury Park conferences began 1826, involving Drummond and Irving). Consistent with 'as early as 1826.'

GC-20-058.rationale: Mourant Brock was a real advent writer, and the '~700 ministers of the Church of England' figure is his own published estimate (as cited by 19th-century advent historians). The attribution is genuine; the figure is not independently verified and is likely a partisan estimate.

GC-20-059.rationale: American advent publications did circulate in Britain and books/journals were reprinted there; documented in Millerite historiography.

GC-20-060.rationale: Corroborated near-verbatim by the local insider source Wellcome: 'In 1842, Robert Winter, an Englishman by birth... fell in with the Adventists in this country' and returned to England. Robert Winter is a documented Millerite emissary to Britain.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874), p.~289 — Robert Winter 1842

GC-20-061.rationale: Winter's advent preaching spread in parts of England with co-labourers; consistent with the insider record.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874)

GC-20-062.rationale: Manuel Lacunza was a Jesuit and a subject of Spain born in Santiago de Chile, who reached a premillennial reading of Scripture. Nuance: he composed his prophetic work in exile in Italy (Imola), not in South America, so the 'in South America... found his way to the Scriptures' framing is imprecise about where the work was done; his origin and identity are correct.

sources.title: Two Hundred Years from Lacunza, JATS (Andrews Univ.)

GC-20-063.rationale: The pseudonym is misstated. Lacunza published as 'Juan Josafat Ben-Ezra' (styled 'Rabbi Juan Josaphat ben-Ezra'), not 'Rabbi Ben-Israel'; the title page of the original ('...J.J. Ben Ezra, 1812') confirms 'Ben-Ezra.' The surrounding claims (a converted-Jew persona, avoiding Roman censure) are accurate; only the name is wrong. Possible source-copying error — flagged for the defender to check White's 19th-century sources for an 'Ben-Israel' rendering.

sources.title: Manuel Lacunza — Wikipedia (pseudonym Juan Josafat Ben-Ezra)
Two Hundred Years from Lacunza, JATS (Andrews Univ.)
La venida del Mesías... J.J. Ben Ezra, 1812 (Internet Archive scan, title page)

GC-20-064.rationale: Lacunza lived in the 18th century (1731–1801). The English translation (by Edward Irving) appeared in 1827; GC's 'about 1825' is close (the manuscript reached and circulated in London in the mid-1820s). Minor date approximation.

GC-20-065.rationale: Johann Albrecht Bengel (1687–1752), Lutheran minister in Württemberg and celebrated biblical scholar/critic, taught the soon coming (his date-setting) in the 18th century. Confirmed.

GC-20-066.rationale: Faithful quotation from a Bengel memoir (footnote 605); consistent with the standard account of his turn to theology and youthful religious struggles.

GC-20-067.rationale: Bengel served in the Württemberg church consistory and held moderate views on toleration; consistent with his biography.

GC-20-068.rationale: Faithful quotation from a Bengel memoir (footnote 606) on toleration; consistent with 067.

GC-20-069.rationale: Specific memoir detail (light on the second coming came while preparing a Revelation 21 Advent-Sunday sermon); drawn from a Bengel biography. Not independently corroborable but consistent with his prophetic focus.

GC-20-070.rationale: Bengel fixed June 18, 1836 for the onset of the millennium/second advent, within a very few years of Miller's 1843/1844. Confirmed.

sources.title: The End-Time Calculation of J.A. Bengel, Journal of Unification Studies (2010)

GC-20-071.rationale: Bengel's writings were widely diffused and his prophetic views were received in Württemberg and beyond in Germany; documented (Württemberg Pietism).

GC-20-072.rationale: Württemberg Pietists influenced by Bengel's date emigrated eastward — to Russia (and the Holy Land) — forming colonies to await the coming; German advent-minded colonies persisted in Russia. Confirmed.

sources.title: Johann Albrecht Bengel — Wikipedia (Swabian emigration eastward)

GC-20-073.rationale: Louis Gaussen preached the second advent at Geneva, the city where Farel and Calvin had earlier established the Reformation. Confirmed.

sources.title: Gaussen, Louis — McClintock & Strong Biblical Cyclopedia

GC-20-074.rationale: Gaussen came of age amid late-18th/early-19th-century rationalism and entered the ministry inclined to skepticism before his evangelical turn; consistent with his biography.

sources.title: Gaussen, Louis — McClintock & Strong Biblical Cyclopedia

GC-20-075.rationale: Reading Rollin's Ancient History drew Gaussen's attention to Daniel 2 and its historical fulfillment, leading to his interest in the second advent. Confirmed.

GC-20-076.rationale: Gaussen chose to teach the prophecies to children first (as Farel had earlier begun in Geneva), hoping to reach the parents; consistent with his own stated method.

sources.title: Gaussen, Louis — McClintock & Strong Biblical Cyclopedia

GC-20-077.rationale: Faithful quotation from Gaussen (footnote 607) explaining why he addressed children first; consistent with his documented method.

GC-20-078.rationale: Adults came to listen as Gaussen taught the children and his church filled; consistent with the accounts of his ministry.

GC-20-079.rationale: Faithful quotation from Gaussen on publishing the children's instruction to shame adults' 'obscurity' pretext; corroborated by the standard account.

GC-20-080.rationale: Faithful quotation of Gaussen's stated desire to popularize prophecy in his congregations.

GC-20-081.rationale: Gaussen was suspended from the ministry, the principal charge being his use of the Bible rather than the official (rationalistic) catechism in instructing youth. Confirmed.

sources.title: Gaussen, Louis — McClintock & Strong Biblical Cyclopedia

GC-20-082.rationale: After suspension Gaussen taught in a theological school (the Evangelical Society's school, from 1834) and continued catechizing children; consistent with his biography.

GC-20-083.rationale: A Scandinavian (chiefly Swedish) advent revival did arise in the early 1840s with widespread interest. source_quality: encyclopedic-only (academic source not located within budget for this sub-cluster; verdict non-accusatory).

GC-20-084.rationale: The Church of Sweden (state church) opposed the lay revival; the Conventicle Act (1726, in force into the mid-1800s) was used to punish unauthorized preachers, and some were confined. Consistent with the record.

GC-20-086.rationale: The Swedish 'ropare' revival was chiefly among the lower classes (peasants, maids, labourers), meeting in humble homes. Consistent with the record.

GC-20-087.rationale: Child preachers, some very young, are a documented feature of the Swedish revival; ages as low as six to eight are reported in the advent-historical literature (Froom) and consistent with the 'child-preacher' character of the movement.

GC-20-090.rationale: [common_knowledge] Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim: the retelling of the Triumphal Entry (crowds hailing Jesus as Son of David, Pharisees asking Him to silence them, Jesus answering that the stones would cry out) accurately reflects Luke 19:37-40 / Matt 21. The embedded Gospel-narrative assertion is accurate; the typological application is theological.

GC-20-091.rationale: William Miller and his co-labourers preached the advent warning in America — the well-documented Millerite movement.

GC-20-094.rationale: Millerite writings were carried abroad (e.g. reprinted in Britain and taken to mission fields); documented.

GC-20-095.rationale: Miller's earlier computation pointed to Christ's coming about the Jewish year ending in the spring of 1844 (around March 21, 1844) before the later 'seventh-month' revision to October 22, 1844. 'Spring of 1844' matches the earlier expectation. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: A. Taylor, The Origins of Millerite Separatism (Aurora Univ.)

GC-20-096.rationale: The message spread state to state with widespread interest through camp meetings and the advent press; documented.

GC-20-097.rationale: Some ministers left their pulpits and salaries to preach the advent (e.g. Charles Fitch, Josiah Litch, and others); documented.

GC-20-098.rationale: Millerite adherents are estimated by historians at roughly 50,000–100,000, so 'many thousands' is well supported. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: A. Taylor, The Origins of Millerite Separatism (Aurora Univ.)

GC-20-101.rationale: General characterization of revival phenomena (confessions, family conversions); consistent with revival accounts but too vague to verify precisely.

GC-20-102.rationale: Adventist meetings drew broad cross-class attendance; consistent with contemporary and historical accounts of the movement's reach.

GC-20-104.rationale: The movement grew rapidly with continual accessions during 1843–44; consistent with the historiography.

GC-20-105.rationale: The setting of a definite time provoked broad opposition from clergy and the irreligious alike; documented, including ridicule in the press.

GC-20-106.rationale: [common_knowledge] Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim: the embedded quotation matches 2 Peter 3:3-4 KJV ('there shall come in the last days scoffers... Where is the promise of his coming?...'). The scriptural text is accurate; the 'fulfillment' framing is theological/unverifiable.

GC-20-107.rationale: [common_knowledge] Control-sample check of a needs_verification:false claim: the Gospel context is accurate — the 'of that day and hour knoweth no man' saying belongs to the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24), which Matthew places after Jesus 'departed from the temple' (24:1) and sat on the Mount of Olives (24:3). Narrative context correctly reported; the exposition is theological.

GC-20-113.rationale: Advent believers were often opposed and ostracized by fellow church members; documented in the 'come-outer' separatism of 1843–44.

sources.title: A. Taylor, The Origins of Millerite Separatism (Aurora Univ.)

GC-20-114.rationale: Churches did expel members over advent belief in 1843–44, which drove the Millerite 'come-out' separation from the denominations. Documented in the separatism scholarship. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: A. Taylor, The Origins of Millerite Separatism (Aurora Univ.)

GC-20-117.rationale: Corroborated by the insider source Wellcome: 'worldly business was for the most part... suspended' as believers prepared for the expected event. Consistent with contemporary accounts.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — business suspended

GC-20-118.rationale: The 'ascension robes' story is largely a hostile-press legend. Millerite papers denied it at the time, the insider historian Wellcome (local) states investigations found 'no instance of the kind anywhere occurred,' and modern Millerite historiography (Nichol; treatments in Numbers & Butler's The Disappointed) regards the robe accounts as exaggerated/fabricated, though a few isolated later claims keep the picture nuanced. White's denial aligns with the dominant scholarly position. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874) — ascension robes 'false and scandalous'
Are the ascension robes a myth? (survey of Millerite historians' debunking)
Great Disappointment — Wikipedia (Millerite denials of robes)

GC-20-120.rationale: Before the expected date, contemporaries did not identify a chronological error in Miller's prophetic reckoning; opponents typically disputed the nature of the predicted event rather than the calculation (see GC-20-121). The concept of a providentially 'covered' error is theological; the empirical part (no acknowledged discovery of a reckoning error) is broadly consistent with the record but hard to establish universally.

GC-20-121.rationale: Attributed to unnamed 'opponents' (footnote 617). Some contemporaries (notably postmillennialists) did accept the prophetic-period chronology while arguing the event foretold was the world's conversion rather than the personal advent — a genuine period position. The specific quotation is not tied to a named individual, so confidence is low.

GC-20-122.rationale: The expected time passed and Christ did not appear — the Great Disappointment (culminating October 22, 1844). Well documented. source_quality: academic-partial.

sources.title: A. Taylor, The Origins of Millerite Separatism (Aurora Univ.)

GC-20-123.rationale: Sincere Millerite believers experienced a bitter disappointment; the episode is literally named the Great Disappointment.

GC-20-124.rationale: Characterization that some professed adherents were driven mainly by fear; plausible and consistent with contemporary observers but a subjective, hard-to-quantify motive claim.

GC-20-125.rationale: After the date failed, some who had professed belief distanced themselves and claimed they had never really expected the advent; consistent with accounts of post-disappointment fallout, though a general characterization.

GC-20-085.rationale: Theological claim that God miraculously sent the message through children; not empirically decidable.

GC-20-088.rationale: Theological claim that the child-preachers were moved by an influence beyond natural gifts; not empirically decidable.

GC-20-089.rationale: Theological claim that ministers acknowledged 'the hand of God'; the supernatural attribution is not empirically decidable.

GC-20-092.rationale: Interpretive claim that America was the center of the Advent Movement; framed as interpretation. (Historically the American Millerite movement was the largest; the load-bearing content here is interpretive.)

GC-20-093.rationale: Prophetic-fulfillment claim (first angel's message fulfilled 'most directly' in America); theological.

GC-20-099.rationale: Interpretive/typological comparison of the preachers to John the Baptist; not empirically checkable.

GC-20-100.rationale: Interpretive contrast between the advent preachers and 'popular pulpits'; rhetorical/theological.

GC-20-103.rationale: Theological claim about the felt presence of holy angels; supernatural, not empirically decidable.

GC-20-108.rationale: Exposition of 1 Thessalonians 5 (the Lord's appearing coming 'unawares'); theological.

GC-20-109.rationale: Interpretive/polemical claim that teachers gave a 'false interpretation' of Scripture; theological judgment.

GC-20-110.rationale: Interpretive characterization of opposing clergy as 'unfaithful watchmen' aiding the deceiver; theological.

GC-20-111.rationale: Interpretive generalization that the humble/devoted received the message first; not empirically checkable as stated.

GC-20-112.rationale: Interpretive claim tying independent Scripture study to acceptance of the advent doctrine; theological/rhetorical.

GC-20-115.rationale: Theological claim about angels watching the results of the warning; supernatural.

GC-20-116.rationale: Theological claim about angels turning away in sadness; supernatural.

GC-20-119.rationale: Theological claim that God designed to prove His people and 'covered a mistake' in the reckoning; not empirically decidable.

GC-20-126.rationale: Theological claim about Jesus and the heavenly host regarding the disappointed; supernatural.

GC-20-127.rationale: Theological claim about angels shielding believers behind the veil; supernatural.
